topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery hummed with a low, expectant murmur. Elias stood near the entrance, his knuckles white where he gripped the strap of his worn messenger bag. He’d spent six months perfecting the series—the charcoal sketches, the layered oils—and now, these vibrant canvases, his very soul poured onto linen, were hanging on the walls. He scanned the room, his eyes darting over the crowd, searching for the familiar splash of cadmium yellow, the jagged line he’d labored over for weeks. When he found it, dominating a central alcove, a tight knot tightened in his chest. He took a slow, deliberate breath, forcing his shoulders to relax, though the tension coiled deep in his spine. Someone, someone had decided this was the moment for the unveiling. He shifted his weight, the polished floor cool beneath his worn boots, and started moving toward the display, a restless energy thrumming just beneath his skin.

[story 2]

"Excuse me," Maya said, her voice pitched slightly too high, cutting through the polite chatter. She stood directly in front of the largest piece—the one with the stormy seascape. Her gaze was fixed on the texture of the paint, lingering with an almost proprietary air. I felt a sudden, sharp prickle behind my eyes. I hadn't even had a chance to properly survey the room; already, an object of mine was being scrutinized, cataloged, claimed. I stopped mid-stride, my jaw tightening. I could feel the heat rising in my neck, a flush spreading across my cheeks. I took a step back, the sound of my shoe scraping against the marble seeming deafeningly loud in the sudden silence of my own head. I needed to speak, needed to assert ownership, but the words caught, lodging like grit in my throat.

[story 3]

The air in the exhibition hall felt thick, heavy with the scent of expensive perfume and new canvas. Julian paced near the periphery, his movements jerky, his hands flexing at his sides. He kept glancing toward the designated area, a taut line pulling at his brow. Every minute that passed felt stretched thin, like an over-tightened wire. He watched as a group of patrons clustered around a particular piece, their murmurs growing louder, more insistent. A low growl vibrated in his throat. He clenched his fists so hard his nails bit into his palms, a silent promise of disruption brewing just beneath the surface of his calm facade. He needed to intervene, needed to reclaim the space, needed to make it clear that this was not a casual viewing.
